feed.title: William Sharp
feed.subtitle: # A simple, whitespace theme for academics. Based on [*folio](https://github.com/bogoli/-folio) design.

entry.title: Biological Network Modeling DevLog 2
entry.content:
DevLog 2, 2026-06-08:

I created a new repository titled “TelluriumProjects” and created a virtual environment within it. I installed Tellurium onto this venv and copied the quickstart prompt in the notebook firstmodel.ipynb View on GitHub discussed both at the end of the first lecture and at Quick Start.

I was surprised by the clarity and intuitiveness of the Antimony syntax. Writing reactions and reaction rates was not difficult, but produced interesting graphs depicting how the species changed. One aspect I am interested in learning more about is the units within these oridnary differential equation systems and the graphs depicting them. Without units to contextualize things, the simulations don’t mean much. I am looking forward to identifying and modeling a real network using experimental rates that give results that can be interpreted in a real life context.

entry.title: Biological Network Modeling DevLog 1
entry.content:
DevLog 1, 2026-06-04:

After jumpstarting the Biological Network Modeling (BNM) project, I found myself wanting to record a log of my learning and progress. This series of blog posts serves to do exactly that.

Hence far, I have mainly sought to determine what I wanted this project to look like. I grew curious about computationally modeling phenomena such as drug toxicology, immune responses, and wound healing, and sought out resources to explore. I setteled on a very brief introduction to ordinary and partial differential equations on youtube before jumping into ENG340/542. The lecture-style videos combined with the introduction to Tellurium (given my previous experince with python and Jupyter Notebooks) felt like a good fit.

I watched the first lecture when determining that this series would be a good fit, and plan to return to follow along with the Tellurium introduction, and practice thinking in terms of (biological) networks.
